news_summary: Recent breakthroughs reveal a cancer vulnerability linked to a DNA repair gene, a pain-relieving cannabis compound without psychoactive effects, and promising advances poised to transform obesity treatment.
news_summary_fr: Des découvertes récentes mettent en lumière une vulnérabilité du cancer liée à un gène impliqué dans la réparation de l'ADN, un composé du cannabis aux propriétés analgésiques mais sans effets psychoactifs, ainsi que des avancées prometteuses susceptibles de révolutionner le traitement de l'obésité.
news_summary_es: Los últimos avances revelan una vulnerabilidad del cáncer relacionada con un gen de reparación del ADN, un compuesto del cannabis con propiedades analgésicas y sin efectos psicoactivos, y avances prometedores que podrían transformar el tratamiento de la obesidad.

articles.title: This DNA repair gene went rogue and exposed a cancer weakness
articles.summary: Scientists have discovered that a gene normally considered a DNA-protecting "good guy" can become dangerous when cells make too much of it. The gene, EXO1, acts like molecular scissors that help repair DNA, but when overproduced it starts cutting DNA it shouldn't, creating damage linked to cancer.
articles.content:
Tumor suppressor genes are often viewed as the body's built-in defense system against cancer. They produce proteins that help maintain and repair DNA, reducing the chances that harmful mutations will accumulate. When these genes stop working properly or are present at low levels, cancer risk can rise.

But new research suggests that having too much of one DNA repair protein can also be a problem.

Researchers at Penn State College of Medicine found that excessive activity of the gene EXO1 can damage DNA rather than protect it. Instead of repairing genetic material, too much EXO1 can break down DNA and destabilize the genome, a key feature of cancer.

The findings, published in Nature Communications, show that EXO1 is overexpressed in 20% to 30% of breast and ovarian cancers, as well as in melanoma, testicular, cervical and hepatobiliary cancers, which occur in the liver, gall bladder and bile duct.

The team also discovered that cancer cells with unusually high levels of EXO1 behave much like cells carrying BRCA mutations, which are well known for increasing the risk of hereditary breast and ovarian cancers. Importantly, these BRCA-like behaviors occurred even when no BRCA mutation was present.

EXO1 May Help Identify Patients for Targeted Therapies

The researchers found that tumors with elevated EXO1 responded to treatments in ways that closely resembled BRCA-mutant cancers.

"EXO1 doesn't predict cancer risk, but it could potentially serve as a biomarker to help predict which patients are more likely to respond to certain chemotherapy treatments, leading to more personalized therapies," said George-Lucian Moldovan, professor of molecular and precision medicine and senior author on the study. "The same drugs that are reserved for treating BRCA-mutant tumors and that have fewer side effects could potentially be used to treat EXO1 overexpressing tumors, which don't have BRCA mutations. It would expand the applicability of those drugs."

To investigate the role of EXO1, the researchers analyzed tumor data from The Cancer Genome Atlas, a National Cancer Institute cancer genomics program. They found evidence of EXO1 overproduction in multiple cancer types, including tumors of the breast, skin, liver and cervix, consistent with earlier research. Elevated EXO1 levels were especially associated with basal-like breast cancer, an aggressive form of the disease.

How Excess EXO1 Damages DNA

The team then performed laboratory experiments using commercially available human cancer cells.

Researchers artificially increased EXO1 production in the cells to determine how excess amounts of the protein affected DNA. They also created a disabled version of EXO1 that produced protein but lacked its normal biochemical activity. This allowed them to confirm that any observed DNA damage was caused by the protein's activity rather than simply its presence.

Under normal conditions, EXO1 functions like a pair of molecular scissors, helping trim and repair damaged DNA. However, when too much EXO1 is present, those scissors begin cutting DNA structures that should remain intact.

The researchers found that excess EXO1 destabilizes newly formed DNA through two main mechanisms, expanding single-stranded DNA gaps and degrading reversed replication forks. Both processes erode DNA and result in localized loss of genetic material, Moldovan explained.

"Regardless of which pathway, EXO1 overexpression leads to the generation and accumulation of toxic lesions in DNA, such as double strand breaks, which we ultimately think is what makes the tumor more sensitive to chemotherapy and increases cell death," said Alexandra Nusawardhana, the lead author of the study and who earned her doctorate in biomedical sciences this year from Penn State College of Medicine.

Why EXO1 Mimics BRCA Mutations

BRCA genes normally produce proteins that help protect vulnerable DNA structures during replication. When BRCA genes are mutated, cells lose part of this protective function, which can contribute to cancer development.

In the current study, however, researchers found that excessive EXO1 activity was able to overwhelm those protective mechanisms even when BRCA genes were functioning normally and carried no mutations.

The team also discovered that EXO1 works alongside another protein called MRE11 to enlarge DNA gaps and generate dangerous DNA breaks.

"Mechanistically, this overexpression does exactly what the loss of the BRCA pathway does in BRCA-mutant tumor cells," Moldovan said.

He noted that EXO1 overexpression differs from BRCA mutations in an important way. It is not inherited, and researchers do not yet know whether it directly causes cancer.

Potential Impact on Cancer Treatment

Because EXO1-overexpressing tumors behaved so much like BRCA-mutant tumors, the researchers investigated whether they would also respond similarly to treatment.

They tested olaparib, a drug commonly used against BRCA-mutant cancers that targets cellular DNA repair pathways. Tumors with elevated EXO1 were highly sensitive to the treatment and responded in a manner similar to BRCA-mutant cancers.

The results suggest that patients whose tumors overexpress EXO1 could potentially benefit from the same repair-targeted therapies, even if they do not carry BRCA mutations.

The researchers also found that EXO1-overexpressing tumors responded to cisplatin, a widely used chemotherapy drug. Their findings raise the possibility that lower doses of cisplatin might achieve comparable tumor shrinkage while reducing side effects.

Because EXO1 overexpression appears in a wider range of tumors than BRCA mutations, Moldovan said it could become a valuable biomarker for guiding treatment decisions.

"We shouldn't treat cancers based on what tissue they come from but based on the landscape of the genetic mutations present in the tumors," Moldovan said. "That would result in high efficiency treatment. That's the future of cancer treatment."

The research team plans to continue studying EXO1 with the long-term goal of launching clinical trials involving patients whose tumors overexpress the gene.

Claudia Nicolae, assistant professor of molecular and precision medicine at Penn State College of Medicine, also contributed to the study.

The work was supported by funding from the National Institutes of Health and Four Diamonds.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about the gene EXO1, which can serve as a biomarker to identify cancer patients who may benefit from targeted therapies similar to those used for BRCA-mutant tumors. This finding has broad implications for improving personalized cancer treatment across multiple cancer types, potentially increasing treatment efficacy and reducing side effects. The research is detailed, focused, and presents a meaningful advancement in cancer therapy.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find gene that reveals new way to treat certain cancers
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Our cells have special genes that help fix damaged DNA and protect us from cancer. One group of these genes, called tumor suppressors, usually prevents harmful changes in DNA. But sometimes, these genes don’t work well, which can lead to cancer.

**What happened** – Researchers at Penn State discovered that a gene called EXO1, which normally helps repair DNA, can become harmful when cells make too much of it. Instead of fixing DNA, too much EXO1 starts cutting DNA in the wrong places, causing damage. This was found in many cancers like breast, ovarian, and skin cancers. Surprisingly, cancer cells with too much EXO1 act like cells with BRCA gene mutations, which are known to increase cancer risk.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because tumors with high EXO1 respond well to treatments usually used for BRCA-mutant cancers. This means patients without BRCA mutations but with extra EXO1 might benefit from these targeted therapies. Using these treatments could be more effective and cause fewer side effects. It also helps doctors know which patients might respond better to certain drugs.

**What's next step** – Scientists want to study EXO1 further and plan to start clinical trials to test if patients with high EXO1 can safely and successfully use these new treatment options. They hope this will lead to more personalized cancer care based on the genes in tumors, not just where the cancer started.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Too much of the DNA repair gene EXO1 can harm DNA and reveal which cancers might respond to specific, more effective treatments usually reserved for BRCA-mutant tumors.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent un gène qui ouvre une nouvelle voie pour traiter certains cancers
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Nos cellules possèdent des gènes spéciaux qui réparent l’ADN endommagé et nous protègent contre le cancer. Un groupe de ces gènes, appelés suppresseurs de tumeurs, empêche normalement les changements nuisibles dans l’ADN. Mais parfois, ces gènes ne fonctionnent pas bien, ce qui peut conduire au cancer.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de Penn State ont découvert qu’un gène appelé EXO1, qui aide normalement à réparer l’ADN, peut devenir dangereux quand les cellules en produisent trop. Au lieu de réparer l’ADN, trop d’EXO1 commence à couper l’ADN aux mauvais endroits, provoquant des dégâts. Cela a été observé dans plusieurs cancers comme ceux du sein, des ovaires et de la peau. Étonnamment, les cellules cancéreuses avec trop d’EXO1 se comportent comme celles avec des mutations du gène BRCA, connu pour augmenter le risque de cancer.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car les tumeurs avec un excès d’EXO1 réagissent bien aux traitements habituellement utilisés pour les cancers BRCA-mutés. Cela signifie que des patients sans mutation BRCA mais avec beaucoup d’EXO1 pourraient bénéficier de ces thérapies ciblées. Ces traitements pourraient être plus efficaces et avoir moins d’effets secondaires. Cela aide aussi les médecins à savoir quels patients répondront mieux à certains médicaments.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques veulent étudier EXO1 plus en détail et prévoient de lancer des essais cliniques pour tester si les patients avec beaucoup d’EXO1 peuvent utiliser ces nouvelles options de traitement en toute sécurité et avec succès. Ils espèrent ainsi personnaliser davantage les soins contre le cancer selon les gènes présents dans les tumeurs, pas seulement selon l’origine du cancer.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Trop d’EXO1, un gène réparateur d’ADN, peut endommager l’ADN et indiquer quels cancers pourraient répondre à des traitements plus efficaces réservés aux tumeurs BRCA-mutées.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren un gen que revela una nueva forma de tratar ciertos cánceres
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Nuestras células tienen genes especiales que ayudan a reparar el ADN dañado y nos protegen contra el cáncer. Un grupo de estos genes, llamados supresores de tumores, normalmente evita cambios dañinos en el ADN. Pero a veces, estos genes no funcionan bien, lo que puede causar cáncer.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de Penn State descubrieron que un gen llamado EXO1, que normalmente ayuda a reparar el ADN, puede volverse dañino cuando las células producen demasiado. En lugar de reparar el ADN, mucho EXO1 empieza a cortar el ADN en lugares equivocados, causando daño. Esto se encontró en varios tipos de cáncer como el de mama, ovario y piel. Sorprendentemente, las células cancerosas con mucho EXO1 actúan como las que tienen mutaciones en el gen BRCA, conocido por aumentar el riesgo de cáncer.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque los tumores con altos niveles de EXO1 responden bien a tratamientos que se usan para cánceres con mutaciones BRCA. Esto significa que pacientes sin mutaciones BRCA pero con mucho EXO1 podrían beneficiarse de estas terapias dirigidas. Usar estos tratamientos puede ser más efectivo y causar menos efectos secundarios. También ayuda a los médicos a saber qué pacientes podrían responder mejor a ciertos medicamentos.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos quieren estudiar más a fondo EXO1 y planean iniciar ensayos clínicos para probar si los pacientes con mucho EXO1 pueden usar estas nuevas opciones de tratamiento de forma segura y exitosa. Esperan así personalizar mejor el cuidado del cáncer basándose en los genes de los tumores, no solo en dónde comenzó el cáncer.

**Resumen en una frase** – Demasiado del gen reparador de ADN EXO1 puede dañar el ADN y mostrar qué cánceres podrían responder a tratamientos más efectivos que normalmente se usan para tumores con mutaciones BRCA.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a delicate pair of golden molecular scissors gently cutting a glowing DNA double helix strand, where one side of the DNA shows fragile breaks and scattered puzzle-like pieces drifting away, set against a soft, warm background of intertwined strands symbolizing genome stability and instability.

articles.title: Scientists found a cannabis compound that relieves pain without the high
articles.summary: Compounds responsible for the aroma of cannabis and many other plants may offer a surprising new way to relieve chronic pain. Researchers found that several cannabis-derived terpenes significantly reduced pain in mouse models of fibromyalgia and post-surgical pain, with one terpene, geraniol, showing especially strong effects. Unlike THC, these compounds are not associated with psychoactive effects, making them a potentially attractive alternative for pain treatment.
articles.content:
Researchers at the University of Arizona Health Sciences have identified compounds from the Cannabis sativa plant that may offer a new way to treat fibromyalgia and post-surgical pain. The findings, published in Pharmacological Reports, add to growing evidence that certain cannabis-derived molecules could help relieve chronic pain without causing the psychoactive effects associated with THC.

The study builds on earlier work from the lab of John Streicher, PhD, a member of the Comprehensive Center for Pain & Addiction, which found that terpenes could reduce pain in models of inflammation and chemotherapy-related nerve damage.

"Our research is showing that terpenes are not a good option for reducing acute pain resulting from an injury, such as stubbing your toe or touching a hot stove; however, we are seeing significant reductions in pain when terpenes are used for chronic or pathological pain," said Streicher, who is a professor in the U of A College of Medicine -- Tucson's Department of Pharmacology. "This study was the first to investigate the impact of terpenes in preclinical models of fibromyalgia and post-operative pain and expand the scope of potential pain-relieving treatments using terpenes."

Cannabis Terpenes Show Strong Pain Relief

Terpenes are natural compounds responsible for the scent and flavor of many plants. In cannabis, they contribute to the plant's distinctive aroma and may also have medicinal properties.

Unlike tetrahydrocannabinol, or THC, terpenes do not produce the unwanted psychoactive effects commonly associated with cannabis. That makes them an appealing area of research for scientists searching for new pain treatments.

For this study, researchers examined four terpenes commonly found in Cannabis sativa: geraniol, linalool, beta-caryophyllene, and alpha-humulene.

In mouse models of fibromyalgia and post-operative pain, all four compounds produced substantial pain-relieving effects. Geraniol delivered the strongest results, followed by linalool, beta-caryophyllene, and alpha-humulene.

New Hope for Fibromyalgia Treatment

Fibromyalgia remains one of the most challenging chronic pain conditions to treat. The disorder affects muscles and soft tissues throughout the body and is estimated to impact up to 5% of the global population, according to research published in Healthcare (Basel) in 2023.

In the United States alone, about 4 million adults have fibromyalgia, according to the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services' Office on Women's Health. Women are affected more often than men.

"With fibromyalgia, there isn't much understanding of what the pain state is, and there are not a lot of great options for treating it," Streicher said. "Our findings show that terpenes may be a viable treatment option for fibromyalgia pain, which could potentially have a large impact and make a difference for an under-treated population."

Potential Alternative for Post-Surgical Pain

The researchers also explored whether terpenes could help with pain following surgery.

Post-surgical pain occupies a unique middle ground between acute and chronic pain. Although it is typically temporary, surgery triggers biological changes including inflammation and increased sensitivity within the body's pain pathways, which can intensify discomfort.

"Opioids do a good job controlling post-surgical pain, but they can cause constipation that can increase the chances of post-surgical complications such as adhesions," Streicher said. "We are always looking for better options, and this study suggests that terpenes could be a novel therapeutic for post-operative pain."

According to research published in the International Journal of Surgery in 2020, roughly 310 million major surgical procedures are performed worldwide each year, highlighting the need for safer and more effective pain-management options.

Natural Compounds Continue to Surprise Scientists

Todd Vanderah, PhD, director of the Comprehensive Center for Pain & Addiction at U of A Health Sciences and professor and head of the Department of Pharmacology at the U of A College of Medicine -- Tucson, said the findings underscore the value of exploring chemicals produced by nature.

"The research that is being done by Dr. Streicher's lab on terpenes and their potential to help those who suffer from chronic pain demonstrates the importance of basic research. There are hundreds of unique chemicals that plants make, including the Cannabis plant, that are undiscovered," Vanderah said.

"Nature is incredible at making unique chemical structures, and many of these chemicals are unknowns when it comes to their abilities to aid in human health, diseases and disorders. A great current example is medication semaglutide, sold under the brand name Ozempic, which has a chemical structure that was isolated not from a plant, but from an animal that is prevalent in the Southwest, the Gila monster. These discoveries from natural products through research such as Dr. Streicher's can result in very useful medications."

How the Terpenes May Work

The team also found that the compounds appeared to act through the same biological pathway identified in previous terpene studies.

Specifically, the pain-relieving effects were linked to the adenosine A2a receptor -- a receptor that caffeine targets and blocks -- suggesting that terpenes may also have sedative properties. Researchers say that possibility warrants further investigation.

The study's co-authors included Caleb Seekins, a former undergraduate biochemistry student in Streicher's lab who is now pursuing a medical degree at the College of Medicine -- Tucson; Alyssa Welborn, who earned a Bachelor of Science in Pharmaceutical Sciences in 2024; and Abigail Schwarz, who completed her doctorate in Streicher's lab in 2024.

Funding for the research was provided by the National Institutes of Health under award no. R01AT011517.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery of cannabis-derived terpenes that relieve chronic pain without psychoactive effects, offering a promising new treatment for fibromyalgia and post-surgical pain. This has broad potential impact for millions suffering from chronic pain worldwide and addresses a major medical challenge with safer alternatives to opioids. The article provides detailed context, study results, and expert commentary, fulfilling criteria for inspiring, substantive good news with meaningful real-world benefits.
articles.personality_title: Scientists discover cannabis compounds that ease pain without causing a high
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context**
Many people with chronic pain conditions like fibromyalgia or pain after surgery need better treatments. Current options often have side effects or don’t work well. Cannabis is known for pain relief but usually comes with a psychoactive “high” caused by THC.

**What happened**
Researchers at the University of Arizona Health Sciences studied natural compounds called terpenes found in cannabis. They tested four terpenes—geraniol, linalool, beta-caryophyllene, and alpha-humulene—on mice with fibromyalgia-like and post-surgery pain. All four reduced pain, with geraniol showing the strongest effect. Unlike THC, these terpenes did not cause any psychoactive effects.

**Impact**
This discovery is important because it points to new pain treatments that don’t cause the “high” linked to cannabis. Fibromyalgia affects millions worldwide and is hard to treat. Also, managing post-surgery pain safely is a big challenge, especially because opioid medicines can cause problems. These terpenes could offer safer alternatives for many people who suffer from long-lasting pain.

**What's next step**
The researchers plan to study exactly how these terpenes work in the body and test their effects in humans. They want to explore if these compounds might also help with sleep or sedation. Further research could lead to new, natural medicines for chronic pain.

**One-sentence takeaway**
Scientists have found cannabis terpenes that relieve chronic pain without the psychoactive effects, offering hope for safer and more effective treatments.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent des composés du cannabis qui soulagent la douleur sans effet planant
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte**
Beaucoup de personnes souffrent de douleurs chroniques comme la fibromyalgie ou la douleur après une opération, et les traitements actuels sont souvent insuffisants ou ont des effets secondaires. Le cannabis est connu pour soulager la douleur, mais provoque généralement un effet planant dû au THC.

**Ce qui s'est passé**
Des chercheurs de l'Université de l'Arizona ont étudié des composés naturels appelés terpènes présents dans le cannabis. Ils ont testé quatre terpènes—géraniol, linalol, bêta-caryophyllène et alpha-humulène—sur des souris souffrant de douleurs similaires à la fibromyalgie et post-opératoires. Tous ont réduit la douleur, avec le géraniol comme plus efficace. Contrairement au THC, ces terpènes ne provoquent pas d’effets psychoactifs.

**Impact**
Cette découverte est importante car elle ouvre la voie à de nouveaux traitements contre la douleur sans les effets planants du cannabis. La fibromyalgie touche des millions de personnes dans le monde et est difficile à traiter. La gestion de la douleur après une chirurgie est aussi un défi, surtout car les opioïdes peuvent causer des problèmes. Ces terpènes pourraient offrir des alternatives plus sûres.

**Prochaine étape**
Les chercheurs souhaitent comprendre comment ces terpènes agissent dans le corps et tester leurs effets chez l’humain. Ils veulent aussi vérifier si ces composés peuvent aider à mieux dormir ou calmer. Ces recherches pourraient aboutir à de nouveaux médicaments naturels contre la douleur chronique.

**Résumé en une phrase**
Des scientifiques ont découvert que les terpènes du cannabis soulagent la douleur chronique sans effet planant, ouvrant la voie à des traitements plus sûrs et efficaces.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren compuestos del cannabis que alivian el dolor sin causar efecto psicoactivo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto**
Muchas personas con dolor crónico, como la fibromialgia o el dolor después de una cirugía, necesitan mejores tratamientos. Las opciones actuales a menudo tienen efectos secundarios o no funcionan bien. Se sabe que el cannabis ayuda a aliviar el dolor, pero generalmente produce un efecto psicoactivo debido al THC.

**Qué pasó**
Investigadores de la Universidad de Arizona estudiaron compuestos naturales llamados terpenos que se encuentran en el cannabis. Probaron cuatro terpenos—geraniol, linalool, beta-cariofileno y alfa-humuleno—en ratones con dolor similar a la fibromialgia y postoperatorio. Todos redujeron el dolor, siendo el geraniol el más efectivo. A diferencia del THC, estos terpenos no causaron efectos psicoactivos.

**Impacto**
Este descubrimiento es importante porque señala nuevos tratamientos para el dolor que no producen el efecto “alto” asociado al cannabis. La fibromialgia afecta a millones en todo el mundo y es difícil de tratar. También, controlar el dolor después de una cirugía es un gran reto, especialmente porque los opioides pueden causar problemas. Estos terpenos podrían ofrecer alternativas más seguras para muchas personas con dolor crónico.

**Próximo paso**
Los investigadores planean estudiar cómo actúan estos terpenos en el cuerpo y probar sus efectos en humanos. Quieren explorar si estos compuestos también podrían ayudar con el sueño o la sedación. Más investigaciones podrían llevar a nuevos medicamentos naturales para el dolor crónico.

**Resumen en una frase**
Científicos han encontrado terpenos del cannabis que alivian el dolor crónico sin efectos psicoactivos, ofreciendo esperanza para tratamientos más seguros y efectivos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a stylized Cannabis sativa leaf intertwined with glowing molecular structures representing terpenes—geraniol, linalool, beta-caryophyllene, and alpha-humulene—radiating gentle light that softly envelops a calm, abstract silhouette of a human torso symbolizing relief from chronic fibromyalgia and post-surgical pain, all rendered in natural, muted greens, soft yellows, and earthy browns.

articles.title: Ozempic changed obesity treatment, but experts say the real revolution is next
articles.summary: The obesity treatment landscape is changing fast, with GLP-1 drugs opening the door to more effective care than ever before. Experts now envision a future where medications, minimally invasive procedures, surgery, and precision medicine work together to deliver better long-term results.
articles.content:
The popularity of GLP-1 medications such as semaglutide (Ozempic, Wegovy, and Rybelsus) and tirzepatide (Mounjaro and Zepbound) has dramatically reshaped the way obesity is treated. However, specialists say these drugs are only one piece of the puzzle when it comes to managing one of the most widespread chronic health conditions in the United States.

A new commentary in the American Gastroenterological Association journal Gastroenterology revisits the influential POWER (Practice Guide on Obesity and Weight Management, Education, and Resources) framework, originally introduced in 2017. Titled "Revisiting POWER in the GLP-1 Age," the update reflects major developments in obesity care, from the arrival of highly effective weight loss medications to advances in endoscopic therapies, broader use of bariatric surgery, and the growing role of precision medicine.

Obesity Treatment Is Expanding Beyond Medication

According to the authors, GLP-1 receptor agonists have significantly changed the obesity treatment landscape. At the same time, they emphasize that obesity care is becoming increasingly comprehensive, combining medications with endoscopic procedures and surgical options when appropriate.

The updated framework also incorporates the emerging concept of clinical obesity. This approach recognizes obesity as a chronic disease that affects multiple body systems and highlights the limitations of relying solely on body mass index (BMI) to evaluate health risks.

Researchers note that obesity treatment is increasingly moving toward a multidisciplinary model that brings together different therapeutic approaches. Rather than viewing medications, procedures, and surgery as separate strategies, experts see them as complementary tools that can be tailored to individual patients.

New Evidence Supports Endoscopic Therapies

The commentary highlights growing evidence for endoscopic bariatric and metabolic therapies, including endoscopic sleeve gastroplasty, as effective treatment options for obesity.

The authors also point to advances in genetics and precision medicine that could help physicians identify which treatments are most likely to benefit specific patients. As researchers learn more about the biological factors that influence obesity, treatment plans may become increasingly personalized.

Another promising area involves combining therapies. Evidence suggests that pairing GLP-1 medications with endoscopic procedures or bariatric surgery could lead to greater weight loss and longer-lasting results than either approach alone.

The Role of Gastroenterologists in Obesity Care

The paper also underscores the important role gastroenterologists and hepatologists play in treating obesity and its related health complications.

These specialists frequently care for patients with obesity-associated conditions, including metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD), gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), gallbladder disease, and a range of gastrointestinal complications linked to obesity. Because of this close involvement, the authors argue that gastroenterologists and hepatologists are well positioned to help guide the future of obesity management as treatment options continue to evolve.
articles.reasoning: The article describes significant advancements in obesity treatment, highlighting the transformative impact of GLP-1 medications and the emerging multidisciplinary approach combining drugs, procedures, surgery, and precision medicine. This represents a meaningful positive development for a widespread chronic health condition, with broad societal benefits and detailed context on evolving treatment strategies.
articles.personality_title: New treatments are reshaping how obesity is managed
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Obesity is a common health problem that affects many people and can lead to other illnesses. Until recently, treatments were limited, but new medicines called GLP-1 receptor agonists have changed how doctors help patients lose weight.

**What happened** – Medications like semaglutide (known as Ozempic or Wegovy) and tirzepatide (Mounjaro) have become popular and show strong results for weight loss. Experts updated a guide called POWER to include these drugs and also new treatments like endoscopic procedures and surgery. They now see obesity care as a mix of medicines, procedures, and personalized plans based on each person's biology.

**Impact** – This is important because it moves obesity treatment beyond just one method. Combining medicines with minimally invasive procedures or surgery can help people lose more weight and keep it off longer. Also, doctors can better match treatments to individuals using new knowledge about genetics and body systems affected by obesity.

**What's next step** – Researchers and doctors will continue to study how to use these treatments together and personalize care even more. Gastroenterologists and liver specialists will play a bigger role in managing obesity and related health problems, guiding the development of better treatments.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Obesity care is becoming more effective by combining new medicines, procedures, and personalized approaches to help people achieve long-term health improvements.
articles.personality_title_fr: De nouveaux traitements transforment la prise en charge de l'obésité
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L'obésité est un problème de santé courant qui peut entraîner d'autres maladies. Jusqu'à récemment, les traitements étaient limités, mais de nouveaux médicaments appelés agonistes des récepteurs GLP-1 ont changé la manière dont les médecins aident les patients à perdre du poids.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des médicaments comme le sémaglutide (connu sous les noms Ozempic ou Wegovy) et le tirzépatide (Mounjaro) sont devenus populaires et montrent de bons résultats pour la perte de poids. Les experts ont mis à jour un guide appelé POWER pour inclure ces médicaments ainsi que de nouveaux traitements comme les procédures endoscopiques et la chirurgie. Ils considèrent désormais la prise en charge de l'obésité comme un mélange de médicaments, de procédures et de plans personnalisés selon la biologie de chaque personne.

**Impact** – Cela est important car cela dépasse une méthode unique. Combiner médicaments, procédures peu invasives ou chirurgie peut aider les personnes à perdre plus de poids et à le maintenir plus longtemps. De plus, les médecins peuvent mieux adapter les traitements grâce aux nouvelles connaissances sur la génétique et les systèmes du corps affectés par l'obésité.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs et médecins continueront d'étudier comment utiliser ces traitements ensemble et personnaliser davantage les soins. Les gastro-entérologues et hépatologues joueront un rôle plus important dans la gestion de l'obésité et des problèmes de santé associés, guidant le développement de meilleurs traitements.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La prise en charge de l'obésité devient plus efficace en combinant nouveaux médicaments, procédures et approches personnalisées pour aider les patients à améliorer leur santé durablement.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevos tratamientos están transformando el manejo de la obesidad
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La obesidad es un problema de salud común que puede causar otras enfermedades. Hasta hace poco, los tratamientos eran limitados, pero nuevos medicamentos llamados agonistas del receptor GLP-1 han cambiado cómo los médicos ayudan a las personas a perder peso.

**Qué pasó** – Medicamentos como semaglutida (conocida como Ozempic o Wegovy) y tirzepatida (Mounjaro) se han vuelto populares y muestran resultados fuertes para la pérdida de peso. Los expertos actualizaron una guía llamada POWER para incluir estos fármacos y también nuevos tratamientos como procedimientos endoscópicos y cirugía. Ahora ven el cuidado de la obesidad como una combinación de medicamentos, procedimientos y planes personalizados según la biología de cada persona.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque va más allá de un solo método. Combinar medicamentos con procedimientos mínimamente invasivos o cirugía puede ayudar a las personas a perder más peso y mantenerlo por más tiempo. Además, los médicos pueden adaptar mejor los tratamientos usando nuevos conocimientos sobre genética y los sistemas del cuerpo afectados por la obesidad.

**Próximo paso** – Investigadores y médicos seguirán estudiando cómo usar estos tratamientos juntos y personalizar la atención aún más. Los gastroenterólogos y especialistas en hígado tendrán un papel más grande en el manejo de la obesidad y problemas relacionados, guiando el desarrollo de mejores tratamientos.

**Conclusión en una frase** – El cuidado de la obesidad es más efectivo al combinar nuevos medicamentos, procedimientos y enfoques personalizados para ayudar a las personas a mejorar su salud a largo plazo.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a balanced scale with symbolic representations of obesity treatment on each side—one side holding stylized capsules and medication bottles labeled with subtle molecular patterns, the other side featuring a delicate, abstract endoscopic tool and a gentle surgical instrument, all set against a soft, natural background blending human anatomy-inspired shapes and DNA helix motifs to convey precision medicine and personalized care.
